title: How to Rent a Boat in Corfu and the Ionian
topic: How to rent a boat in Corfu and the Ionian
html:
The Ionian Sea has a reputation for gentle conditions and impossibly clear water, which is exactly why so many people choose this corner of Greece for a day afloat. Corfu sits at the northern edge of the island chain, within easy reach of small coves, sheltered bays and a handful of neighbouring islands that feel a world away from the busy waterfront. If you are weighing up how to get out on the water here, the good news is that the practical side is far simpler than most first-timers expect. This guide walks through the choices, the costs and the small details that make the difference between a smooth outing and a stressful one.

Why the Ionian suits a relaxed day on the water

Unlike the Aegean, where the meltemi wind can churn the sea for days, the Ionian tends to stay calm through the summer. Mornings are usually glassy, with a light afternoon breeze that suits both engines and sails. That stability is a gift for anyone new to boating, and it means families and mixed-ability groups can plan an itinerary without constantly checking the forecast. When you decide to rent a boat in this region, you are buying into water that forgives a slow learning curve.

Choosing the right type of boat

The first real decision is what you want to be sitting on. Your options usually fall into a few clear groups.

  • Small open boats with modest outboard engines are perfect for a short hop to a nearby beach and rarely need a licence.
  • RIBs and speedboats cover ground quickly and suit those who want to reach several coves in a single day.
  • Sailing yachts and catamarans reward anyone with experience and a love of slow travel.
  • Motor cruisers bridge comfort and pace for larger groups who want shade and a proper cabin.

If you are unsure which suits your group, browsing a few options side by side helps the decision settle. A quick look at a boat rental marketplace lets you compare layouts, capacities and prices in one place rather than emailing operators one at a time.

Licence rules and skipper options

Greek regulations allow many smaller craft to be hired without a formal licence, provided the engine power stays below a set threshold. Step up to a larger or faster vessel and you will need a recognised certificate of competence, or you can simply hire a skipper for the day. Bringing a local skipper on board is often the smartest call for visitors. They know the safe anchorages, the spots that catch the afternoon swell, and the tavernas worth stopping for. It also frees you to relax rather than worry about navigation.

A half day with a skipper teaches you more about local waters than a week of reading charts, and it usually costs less than people fear.

Where to go from Corfu

From the main harbour and the northern resorts, several routes stand out. The west coast hides dramatic limestone cliffs and the famous turquoise pools near Paleokastritsa. Head south and you can chase quiet swimming stops along the protected channel between Corfu and the mainland. Confident crews sometimes push across to Paxos and Antipaxos, where the sea takes on an almost tropical colour, though that crossing deserves respect and a watchful eye on the wind. Plan your stops loosely, leave time for lunch at anchor, and you will not want the day to end. When you hire a boat for a full day, this slower rhythm is the whole point.

What it costs and how to book well

Prices swing with season, size and whether a skipper is included. A small self-drive boat for the day can be very affordable, while a crewed yacht in peak August will sit at the top end. Fuel is sometimes extra, so confirm that before you commit. Read the deposit terms, check the cancellation policy against the weather, and make sure the safety gear and insurance are spelled out clearly. Booking ahead in July and August is essential, as the best boats vanish quickly. Many travellers now arrange everything through a single yacht charter platform so the paperwork and payment sit in one trusted place.

A note for boat owners

If you keep a boat in the Ionian and only use it a handful of weeks each year, the same demand that drives visitors here can work in your favour. Owners can list a vessel, set their own availability and turn idle months into income. The model is straightforward and the audience is already searching, so it is worth considering whether your boat could pay for some of its own upkeep.
